checks.a_analysis: The output accurately reflects the content of the source PDF. I have compared every section, including the cover page, table of contents, introductory text, data specification tables, and appendices. There are no invented sentences or paragraphs. All text in the JSON has a clear basis in the source document.

The source document contains no phone numbers, email addresses, or specific URLs. The output correctly reflects this absence and does not hallucinate any contact information. The references on the final page are listed correctly as they appear in the source.

All 14 pages of the source PDF are accounted for in the output JSON. This includes the cover page, the table of contents, the technical specification sections, and the appendices (Appendix A and B). No pages or sections were dropped.

I have cross-verified the row counts for all tables. The data specification table in Appendix A (split across pages 4-8) is fully represented. The NC Counties table on page 9 and the extensive Place Type table (spanning pages 9-13) are also complete. Specifically, the Place Type table contains all 54 category rows plus the header, matching the source content exactly.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The text within each section is complete. Descriptions in the tables and the introductory text on page 3 match the source. Even minor elements like the 'Expected Use' note on page 8 and the 'Placement' list on page 13 are fully captured.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content matches the source document faithfully. OCR accuracy is high, and there are no wording changes that alter the technical meaning of the data standards. Reformatting of list structures and tables for the JSON schema is appropriate and consistent.

checks.a_analysis: The output document follows the logical progression of the source PDF from the cover page through the Table of Contents, body text, appendices, and references. No instances of duplicated paragraphs or sections were found. The repetition of the document title at the start of the body (Page 3) reflects the intentional structure of the source.

The heading hierarchy is preserved and logical. The document uses H1 for the main title and section starts (Contents, Body), H2 for major sections (Preface, Introduction, Appendices), and H3 for subsections (Coordinate System, NC Counties, etc.). There are no instances of skipped levels (e.g., H1 to H3).

Tables in the document are correctly converted. Although large tables (like Appendix A and Place Type) are split into multiple table objects in the JSON based on page breaks, they maintain consistent column counts (7 for Appendix A, 3 for Place Type, 6 for NC Counties) and correctly repeat header rows for accessibility. Data alignment between headers and cells is accurate.
The reading order in the output faithfully follows the source document's flow, beginning with the cover page elements and proceeding through the TOC and body sections in the correct sequence.

The 'References' section on page 14 is presented as a numbered list (1-8) in the source PDF. In the output (element index 135), it has been converted to an unordered list, and the item numbers have been stripped entirely, resulting in a loss of structural information.

checks.a_analysis: Several hyperlinks present in the source PDF are missing in the output. This includes references in the Preface (e.g., 'member listing1'), multiple standard body and domain links within the Appendix A tables (e.g., 'NENA', 'USPS', 'NC Counties', 'AddressNC Place Type Domain'), and the external links in the final References section. These are rendered as plain text without link metadata.
checks.b_evidence_source: Hyperlinks for 'NENA', 'USPS', 'NC Counties' in Appendix A tables (pages 4, 5, 7) and 'member listing1' in Preface (page 3).
checks.b_evidence_output: The text segments for these items do not contain any link or anchor metadata.

checks.a_analysis: The Appendix A - Fields table in the source PDF is presented as a single continuous table across five pages (pages 4-8), with fields listed in a specific logical sequence (e.g., address components, then administrative areas, then census fields, then coordinates). In the converted HTML, this table has been entirely reorganized: the content is split into numerous smaller tables grouped by the first letter of the Field Name and sorted alphabetically. This significantly changes the visual organization and flow of information, disrupting the original logical grouping of the data schema. While the information is present, it is no longer in the same relative location or sequence as the original standard.
checks.b_evidence_source: The Appendix A - Fields table starting on page 4 of the PDF follows a sequential order: Add_Number, AddNum_Suf, St_PreMod, St_PreDir... with Addtl_Loc appearing later on page 6.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output reorganizes Appendix A into alphabetical sections. Under heading 'A', 'Addtl_Loc' is now placed immediately after 'AddNum_Suf'. The street name components (St_...) are moved far down the list under heading 'S'.
checks.b_evidence_location: Appendix A - Fields (starting at page 4 in PDF)

checks.a_analysis: All tables in the document, including the complex tables in Appendix A and B, are rendered with clear boundaries, distinguishable rows and columns, and consistent header alignment. Although Appendix A is split and reorganized, each resulting table segment is visually intact and the data remains readable and understandable. Headers are repeated for each segment, ensuring context is maintained for the user.

The primary meaningful image in the source document is the map of North Carolina on the cover page (page 1), which illustrates the density of address points. This image is successfully rendered in the HTML output. The 'DRAFT' watermark present in the PDF background is correctly omitted in the HTML output, as decorative images and background patterns are typically hidden for accessibility improvements.

There is no evidence of duplicated body content. Each paragraph, heading, and table entry appears exactly once in the main content area. The appearance of headings in the navigation sidebar (Table of Contents) as well as the main body is an intentional accessibility feature and does not constitute duplication.
